pages.text:
635
Epidemiology and Physiologic Changes  
of Aging
People are living longer worldwide. Consequently, countries are 
experiencing population growth in total number as well as the 
proportion of older adults. It is projected that by 2030, one in six 
people in the world will be aged 60 years or over.57 Between 2015 
and 2050, the proportion of the world’s population over 60 years 
old will nearly double from 12% to 22%. There are numerous 
health conditions and physiologic changes associated with aging. 
As people age, their body undergoes changes in different areas 
often at the same time. 57 The details of the physiologic changes 
associated with aging are summarized in Table 31.1. Mobility 
decline with aging is an essential area to address in maintaining a 
person’s independence and is one area in which physiatrists are 
often consulted. Sarcopenia (reduction in muscle mass, strength, 
and power) is one of the main culprits of mobility decline, which 
is elaborated on in the next section.
Sarcopenia
Sarcopenia is a progressive and generalized skeletal muscle disorder 
that is associated with an increased likelihood of adverse out -
comes, including falls, fractures, physical disability, and mortal -
ity.43 Sarcopenia has been an exciting area of basic and clinical 
research due to its predictability of adverse outcomes, its respon -
siveness to treatment, and the associated cost. Sarcopenia is  
• Medication reconciliation should take place during every en -
counter as an opportunity to deprescribe, downtitrate, or fur -
ther educate a patient about the medication’s proper usage.
• Delirium can be prevented or addressed early through im -
proved recognition by all members of the care team.
• Mobility goals should be determined using realistic expecta -
tions for each individual patient according to their own goals 
for activity and quality of life.
• Providers who regularly take care of geriatric patients will often 
be challenged with biomedical ethics conflicts. These providers 
should work to understand the basic principles of biomedical 
ethics and how to utilize an ethical decision-making model.
31
Geriatrics
MOOYEON OH-PARK, HARIS CHOUDRY, AND MALAV SHAH
• By 2030, one in six people in the world will be age 60 years or 
older. Physiatrists can play a large role in maintaining function 
and independence of older individuals.
• Sarcopenia is a progressive and generalized skeletal muscle dis-
order associated with increased likelihood of adverse outcomes. 
The diagnosis of sarcopenia is based on muscle strength (hand 
grip ,27 kg for males, ,16 kg for females), muscle quality, and 
physical performance (e.g., gait speed ,0.8 m/sec).
• Falls in the elderly can have a single or multifactorial cause. Preven-
tion is the best strategy, but it is important to thoroughly investi-
gate possible contributing factors and address them after a fall.
• The Institute for Healthcare Improvement developed the Age-
Friendly Health Systems Initiative to guide geriatric care using 
the 4Ms: Medication, Mentation, Mobility, What Matters to You.
SUMMARY PEARLS
formally listed as muscle disease with a 2024 International Clas -
sification of Diseases, T enth Revision, Clinical Modification code 
M62.84 (icd10data.com). However, there has been a lag in the 
implementation of recognition and treatment of sarcopenia in 
clinical practice. The focus of practice should be on preventing, 
delaying, treating, and even reversing sarcopenia by systematic 
screening and interventions with the aims of improving mobility 
and decreasing falls, hospitalizations, and deaths.
Several organizations and scientific societies have developed 
their own sarcopenia definitions specific to their regions. T o de -
velop an international consensus on the definition, the Global 
Leadership Initiative in Sarcopenia (GLIS) was formed in 2019 to 
2021.26 The GLIS has developed a conceptual definition of sarco-
penia, which will be used to formulate an operational definition 
of the term. GLIS agreed that muscle strength, muscle-specific 
strength (muscle strength normalized to the cross-sectional area of 
a muscle or muscle group), and muscle mass are components  
of sarcopenia, and physical performance (e.g., gait speed) is the 
outcome rather than a component of sarcopenia. Until a new 
operational definition by GLIS is available, the definition from 
the European Working Group on Sarcopenia in Older People  
2 (EWGSOP2) revised in 2018 and published in 2019 is appli -
cable.13 The revised definition from EWGSOP2 focuses on muscle 
function rather than muscle mass based on the better predictabil-
ity of muscle function on adverse outcomes compared to muscle 
mass. The EWGSOP2 cutoff points are as follows: (1) muscle 
strength: five times chair rise .15 sec or hand grip strength

SECTION 3  Common Clinical Problems
TABLE 
31.1 Age-Related Changes in Multiple Organs and Systems
Organ/System Age-Related Changes
Musculoskeletal  
system
1.5%–2% decline in muscle strength/year after age 40 years
Preferential loss of type II fibers
Decline in muscle power more pronounced than muscle strength
Bone changes: bone mineral density loss, postural changes (see more details in the text)
Neurologic system Depletion of neurons in brain, loss of anterior horn cells
Decreased nerve conduction velocities
Cardiovascular  
system
Cardiac output decreases after third decade at a rate of 1%/year
Causes for ↓ in cardiac output: ↓ in inotropic response to catecholamines, ↑in myocardial stiffness, and progressive stiffening of 
the aorta (↑afterload)
Decrease in aerobic capacity (↓VO
2 max)
Respiratory system Linear ↓ in vital capacity: males (26 mL/year), females (22 mL/year) after age 20 years; residual volume (RV) ↑ with age
Total lung capacity (TLC) remains constant
RV/TLC ↑ from 20% at age 20 years to 35% at age 60 years
↑ in alveolar-arterial oxygen difference O
2 due to mismatch of ventilation and perfusion
Predisposition to pneumonia due to depression of immune system function, aspiration of oropharyngeal secretions, slower  
mechanical clearing of tracheobronchial tree by mucociliary apparatus, less effective cough
Kidney/urinary  
system
↓ in number of glomeruli (1,000,000 at age 30 years to 700,000 by age 65 years)
↓ in creatinine clearance
↓ in glomerular filtration rate: 10%/10 years after age 30 years
No change in serum creatinine concentration with age due to age-related ↓ in creatinine production (↓ in muscle mass)
Drugs cleared by glomerular filtration (e.g., aminoglycosides, digoxin, penicillin, tetracycline) may have prolonged half-life even if 
the dosage is adjusted based on serum creatinine concentration .
↓ in ability to concentrate and dilute urine: ↑ vulnerability for dehydration and hyponatremia.
Urinary incontinence (17% of males, 23% of females age .65 years), and overflow incontinence due to possibly reduced and  
delayed sensation of urge to urinate
Gastrointestinal  
system
Presbyesophagus: ↓ in peristaltic response, ↑ in nonperistaltic response, a delayed transit time
↓ in relaxation of the lower sphincter during swallowing; may lead to dysphagia and reduced calorie consumption
Atrophic gastritis (40% of those age $65 years): risk of achlorhydria, deficient intrinsic factor secretion, ↓ pepsinogen production
Hypotonic colon: longer stool transit time, dehydration, which eventually causes chronic constipation
Loss of control in internal and external anal sphincters
Prevalent diverticulosis (50% in those age $80 years)
Liver function shows little change with aging, yet drugs (e.g., diazepam) are metabolized more slowly with aging
Endocrine system ↓ in number and function of insulin-producing beta cells
More inactive precursor proinsulin released in response to a glucose challenge and development of progressive peripheral insulin 
resistance with age
↓ in lean body mass with relative ↑in adiposity (↑ in fat cell size, not in number of fat cells)
Sexual function Males produce less sperm, sexual responses are delayed by age 50 years. Impotence in males is not normal part of aging.
Females after menopause have ↓ in size of uterus and ovaries and ↓ in vaginal secretion
Skin The turnover rate of cells in the stratum corneum ↓ with age (e.g., 50% longer to reepithelialize blistered skin at age 65 years 
compared to the young)
↓ in epidermal cell growth and division and ↓in number of dermal blood vessels contributes to risk of pressure ulcers
Less activity of sweat glands, hydration is difficult to assess by skin alone
Hearing Presbycusis: high-frequency sounds become harder to hear along with difficulty in distinguishing the changes in tone and speech 
due to loss of hair cells and fibrous changes in the cochlea
Difficulty having a conversation in large, crowded rooms with background noise
Conductive hearing loss from middle or outer ear canal (e.g., cerumen accumulation in external canal)
Eyes/vision Dry eyes from ↓ tear production
Three common disease conditions (cataract, glaucoma, macular degeneration–central vision loss)
,27 kg and ,16 kg for males and females, respectively;  
(2) muscle quantity: appendicular skeletal muscle mass/height 2 
,7.0 kg/m2 and ,5.5 kg/m2 for males and females, respectively; 
and (3) physical performance: gait speed ,0.8 m/sec and short 
physical performance battery (SPPB) #8 score.
Sarcopenia increases the risk of falls and fractures, impairs 
mobility and the ability to perform activities of daily living 
(ADLs), contributes to loss of independence, and increases mor -
tality. Sarcopenia is also associated with cardiorespiratory disease 
and cognitive decline. The estimated social and economic costs

637Chapter 31  Geriatrics
associated with muscle weakness was ₤2707 per person per year 
in the United Kingdom36 and twofold higher for those with sar -
copenia in the Czech Republic.47 Individuals with sarcopenia had 
five times higher cost of care during hospitalization compared to 
those without.
8
The pathophysiology underlying sarcopenia is still being dis -
covered. Skeletal muscle is the largest reserve of protein in  
the body and adapts its mass and function to both physiologic 
and pathologic stimuli.
31 Mitochondrial metabolic dysfunction, 
inflammation, and apoptosis were identified as contributors to 
sarcopenia. Sestrins were recognized as metabolic regulators that 
affect antioxidant functions and bring about metabolic benefits by 
nutritional and environmental cues along with cell growth, me -
tabolism, and inflammation. There is increasing evidence that 
sarcopenia actually originates in early life, including the decline in 
grip strength across the lifespan.
14
In spite of the serious impact on health outcomes of sarcopenia, 
recognition in clinical practice is suboptimal. Most clinicians are 
unaware of the sarcopenia concept or the guidelines for its diagno-
sis and treatment. The strength, assistance with walking, rising from 
a chair, climbing stairs, and falls (SARC-F) questionnaire is a vali-
dated patient-reported screening tool used to detect signs and 
symptoms of sarcopenia (Table 31.2).
29 The SARC-F questionnaire 
has five questions asking about difficulty in performing mobility 
and ADLs with a possible score range of 0 to 10. The recommended 
cutoff value is 4 points or more for identifying people who are at 
risk of sarcopenia-associated adverse outcomes.
29
The mainstay of interventions for sarcopenia are resistance 
exercise (RE) and optimizing nutritional intake. RE is shown to 
improve function significantly. The effectiveness of RE in improv-
ing body composition outcomes, however, is less impressive. The 
prescription of RE is based on specificity, overload, and progres -
sion. Monitoring the individual’s response using rate of perceived 
exertion (0–10 scale) is essential, starting with a score of 3 to  
5 and progressing to 6 to 8. 23 Exercise frequency is usually twice 
a week; however, some individuals may need to start once a week 
due to a low fitness level. Each session may include two to three 
sets of 6 to 12 repetitions for lower limb muscle groups or upper 
limb muscle groups. Intensity of exercise of initial training can be 
30% to 60% of 1 repetition maximum (1RM) and advanced to 
50% to 70% of 1RM or even higher. The rest interval between 
training sessions is a minimum of 48 hours. The duration of the 
exercise program to bring clinical effectiveness is at least 12 weeks. 
There are barriers identified for older adults to participate in RE 
programs. The key is to start gently for older adults to experience 
the benefits of RE without being put off by pain, fatigue, and 
negative impressions of RE.
11
Future directions include the use of routine health data, pre -
scription of REs, translation of biologic interventions in human 
subject studies, and expansion of the interventions in low- and 
middle-income countries through global leadership initiatives on 
sarcopenia. GLIS has been supported by the Asian Working 
Group for Sarcopenia, EWGSOP , Sarcopenia Definitions and 
Outcomes Consortium, and 15 major organizations and societies 
with interest in the field.
Frailty
Frailty is a multidomain and dynamic condition defined as a state 
of increased vulnerability resulting from age-associated declines in 
reserve and function across multiple physiologic systems, such 
that the ability to cope with everyday or acute stressors is compro-
mised.
19 The components of the Fried frailty phenotype opera -
tional definition have been widely used. The five components are:
• U nintentional weight loss ( $10 pounds in the last year)
• S elf-reported exhaustion
• W eakness (grip strength)
• S low gait speed
• Lo w level of physical activity
Each component is scored 0 for no and 1 for yes, with the 
score ranging from 0 to 5. T otal score of 0 is categorized as being 
robust, 1 to 2 as prefrail, and 3 or more as frail.
Osteoporosis and Frailty Fracture
Osteoporosis is a skeletal disease characterized by low bone mass 
and microarchitectural deterioration of bone tissue with a conse -
quent increase in bone fragility and susceptibility to fracture. Type 
I osteoporosis is seen in females and is thought to be estrogen-
dependent accelerated bone loss after menopause. In this situa -
tion, resorption exceeds the rate of bone formation, possibly due 
to increased cytokine production and subsequent promotion of 
osteoclastic activities in the estrogen-deficient state. Type II osteo-
porosis occurs with aging and affects both sexes. Here, the 
mechanism is due to the decreased number and activity of osteo -
blasts, which leads to decreased bone formation.
Osteoporosis can be defined as reduced bone strength and 
bone mass per unit volume that increases risk for fracture second-
ary to little or no trauma. The degree of osteoporosis is defined by 
bone mineral density (BMD), which is a value from a test measur-
ing the amount of calcium in a particular region of bone in g/cm
3. 
This value estimates the strength of bone and the amount of bone 
mass. Among the ways to measure BMD are dual x-ray absorpti -
ometry scan, quantitative computed tomography, quantitative 
ultrasound, and iliac crest bone biopsy. The World Health

638
SECTION 3  Common Clinical Problems
Organization’s definition of osteoporosis is BMD 2.5 standard 
deviations (SD) below the average value for a healthy young 
adult.56 Osteopenia is categorized as 1.0 to 2.5 SD below the aver-
age. A T-score is used for a female in postmenopause or a male 
aged 50 years or older and is the difference between the patient’s 
BMD and zero, which is the BMD of a healthy young adult. 51 A 
Z-score is used for premenopausal females or males younger than 
50 years. It is the difference between the patient’s BMD and the 
average BMD for healthy people of similar age, ethnicity, and sex. 
Osteoporosis is one of the most common bone diseases in humans. 
Worldwide, 9 million fractures due to osteoporosis occur annually. 
One in three females and one in five males over age 50 years will 
experience osteoporotic fractures. 56 Approximately one-third of 
patients with a hip fracture will fracture the opposite hip.
Indications for BMD screening from the US Preventive Ser -
vices Task Force are:
• Females age 65 years and older should be screened routinely 
for osteoporosis (grade B recommendation).
• Postmenopausal females younger than 65 years at increased 
risk of osteoporosis should be screened for osteoporosis. 52
Osteoporotic or frailty fractures have varying definitions but 
can be thought of as fractures that occur in individuals with low 
BMD.56 Common sites of osteoporotic fractures are the hip, 
spine, pelvis, sacrum, and humerus. Risk factors for osteoporotic 
fractures include falls, advanced age, dementia, reduced physical 
activity, female sex, and White race. Factors contributing to in -
creased risk for osteoporotic fractures include low BMD, previous 
osteoporotic fracture, increasing age, maternal history of fracture, 
certain medications, small body habitus, sedentary lifestyle, 
menopause, and estrogen levels.56
Osteoporosis is classified as either primary or secondary osteo-
porosis. The classification is based on factors affecting the metabo-
lism of bone. Primary osteoporosis is osteoporosis that develops as 
a result of aging or menopause-related bone demineralization. Type 
I/postmenopausal and type II/senile osteoporosis are two subtypes 
of primary osteoporosis. Secondary osteoporosis is the result of 
medications or pathologic conditions other than aging and meno-
pause that lead to decreased bone mass and increased fracture risk.3
Laboratory tests are often ordered to exclude secondary causes 
of osteoporosis in patients. Recommended laboratory tests for 
evaluating osteoporosis include complete blood count, thyroid-
stimulating hormone, vitamin D, creatinine, hepatic transami -
nase, calcium, phosphorus, alkaline phosphatase, albumin, eryth-
rocyte sedimentation rate, and 24-hour calcium excretion level. 46
Nonpharmacologic interventions to address osteoporosis tar -
get diet, exercise, smoking cessation, reduction of alcohol intake, 
and maintenance of an ideal body weight.56 Common pharmaco-
logic treatments are bisphosphonates, selective estrogen receptor 
modulators, calcitonin, parathyroid hormone, estrogen replace -
ment therapy, hormone therapy, denosumab, fluoride, calcium, 
and vitamin D. The FRAX algorithm predicts the 10-year fracture 
probability using numerous factors and information. It considers 
the patient’s country, BMD, age, sex, and clinical risk factors (e.g., 
personal history of fracture, low body mass index, use of oral 
glucocorticoid therapy, rheumatoid arthritis, osteoporosis second-
ary to another condition, parental history of hip fracture, current 
smoking, and alcohol intake of three or more drinks per day).18,35
Falls Prevention and Intervention
Falls are defined as a loss of upright position that results in down-
ward displacement of the body to the floor or hitting another 
object.35 Falls are a significant issue for older adults. It is estimated 
that one of three older adults age 65 years or older falls each year. 
By 80 years of age, over half of older adults fall annually. 55 Falls 
have direct costs, such as health care costs, and indirect costs, such 
as societal productivity. Hospital inpatient costs account for over 
half of the cost of falls, followed by long-term care costs. Indirectly, 
falls can cause lost productivity of the individual and family/  
caregivers, leading to lost income. Falls are often multifactorial 
(Fig. 31.1) but can be broken down into the following categories: 
environmental, physical, mental, and pharmacologic. Table 31.3 
shows examples of interventions for the different categories.
Arthritis and Joint Replacement
Osteoarthritis is the most common musculoskeletal condition 
that causes joint pain, and as the population ages, it becomes 
more prevalent. Of adults age 65 to 74 years, 44% have arthritis, 
and 53.9% of adults aged 75 years and older are found to have 
arthritis.15 T reatment is initially conservative, but many geriatric 
patients will eventually receive a joint replacement. Conservative 
management consists of nonsurgical treatment options such as 
patient education, weight loss, therapeutic exercise, assistive de -
vices, and orthoses. It also consists of pharmacologic interven -
tions, such as oral pain medications, topical pain medications, 
joint injections, nerve blocks, and ablations. 4
Patients who have failed conservative management and are 
good surgical candidates will often proceed to joint replacement. 
Surgical indications include failure of conservative treatment with 
severely limiting joint pain, decreased mobility, and decreased 
quality of life. Surgical contraindications include infection, medi-
cal risk factors, osteoporosis, dementia, young age, and limb 
weakness. Elderly patients should be counseled appropriately on 
the risks associated with surgical intervention. Compared to 
younger patients, older total hip arthroplasty and total knee ar -
throplasty recipients were more likely to experience postoperative 
complications, admission to the intensive care unit, discharge to 
a skilled care facility, and longer hospital length of stay.16 Patients 
should undergo a thorough preoperative assessment, including a 
close assessment of their cardiopulmonary status. Perioperatively, 
optimal control of medical comorbidities is needed. Postopera -
tively, early mobilization, therapy, and pain control can signifi -
cantly impact outcomes. 16 Postoperative rehabilitation is key to 
recovery after surgery. It can occur in various settings and intensi-
ties. Options include acute inpatient rehabilitation, subacute re -
habilitation, home therapy, and outpatient therapy. It has been 
shown that similar results can be achieved in all settings with 
proper resources and coordination of care.28
Neurologic Disorders Affecting Gait  
and Balance
Human walking is a complex interplay of nervous, musculoskel -
etal, and cardiorespiratory systems and requires cognitive execu -
tive function for planning along with continuous interaction with 
the environment.37,44 T esting walking during dual tasking shows 
the taxing effect of cognition on gait in older adults and individu-
als with central nervous system disorders. Gait speed is often 
measured as a quantitative metric of abnormal gait. Gait speed 
declines by 1% per year from 60 to 80 years of age. 9 Slow gait 
speed has been shown to predict functional decline, dementia, 
and mortality in older adults. The prevalence of abnormal gait is

639Chapter 31  Geriatrics
TABLE 
31.3 Fall Risk Assessment and Interventions
Category Intervention
Environmental Home assessment, floor mats, hip protectors, self-
locking wheelchairs
Mental Cognition screens such as Mini-Mental State Exami-
nation, Geriatric Depression Scale, and Montreal 
Cognitive Assessment
Physical Hearing and vision screens, vitamin levels, footwear, 
compression stockings; gait and balance assess-
ment, Tinetti fall eval, Morse fall risk assessment, 
stratify fall risk assessment, exercise, range of 
motion/flexibility, balance exercises
Pharmacologic Screening, side effects, Beers criteria, reduce key 
classes where possible, antihypertensives, diuret-
ics, pain medications, antiepileptics, antidepres-
sants, antipsychotics, steroids, medication reviews
32.2% among adults aged 60 to 97 years, one-quarter of which 
were neurologic gait abnormalities. 27 However, 38.3% of those 
aged 80 years or older still had normal gait. Therefore gait disorders 
are not an inevitable part of aging. The etiologies underlying the 
development of abnormal gait include neurologic, musculoskeletal, 
medical (e.g., cardiopulmonary, obesity), and medication-related 
issues. T reatment approaches aim to address the underlying dis-
ease with medical and operative interventions combined with gait 
training, assistive devices, and education regarding falls.
There are a number of specific abnormal gait patterns among 
older adults. Hypokinetic rigid gait is characterized by shuffling 
with short step length, a stooped posture, and turning en bloc. 
Management includes optimizing medications targeting specific 
conditions if present (e.g., Parkinson disease), addressing ortho -
static hypotension, and prescribing rehabilitation programs. 
Spastic gait can be hemiparetic or paraparetic. Spastic hemipa -
retic gait is caused by unilateral brain dysfunction with typical 
extensor synergy pattern, circumduction of the swinging leg, and 
slow gait speed. Spinal cord lesions, including cervical myelopa -
thy from spondylosis, may cause spastic paraparetic gait. Ataxic 
gait can be from cerebellar lesions or proprioceptive sensory dis -
turbances. Cautious gait refers to an excessive degree of age-  
related change in walking and fear of falling. Walking difficulties 
are often out of proportion to the level of sensory and motor 
dysfunction. Gait often improves with very minimal support, 
including touching of the patient’s hand.
37 Medication use can 
cause gait impairment, including antidepressants (selective sero -
tonin reuptake inhibitors and tricyclic antidepressants), benzodi-
azepines, analgesics, anticholinergics, and anticonvulsants.
44 
Medication reconciliation should be a mandatory part of evalua -
tion of gait disorders in older adults. Table 31.4 summarizes the 
gait characteristics and underlying causes of common gait disor -
ders in older adults.

BPPV, Ménière’s disease

at the end) not worn through

Age-Friendly Care in Rehabilitation
Age-friendly care has become an area of focus throughout the medi-
cal field as the proportion of Americans over the age of 65 years 
continues to grow. The 2020 US census data demonstrated that 
16.8% of the population was over age 65 years, representing about 
55.8 million people. This population is expected to increase to 
about 95 million individuals by 2060.
49 Health systems have 
sought to determine a guideline approach to caring for older adults 
throughout the continuum of medical care, and physiatry plays a 
crucial role in this care. The Institute for Healthcare Improvement 
(IHI) has developed the Age-Friendly Health Systems initiative, 
which seeks to recognize institutions that provide evidence-based 
care to the geriatric population within a framework of the 4Ms: 
Medication, Mentation, Mobility, and What Matters to You.
24 
This framework can be a complementary guide for the physiatric 
care of older adults in both inpatient and outpatient settings.
Medication Usage
Metabolism
With increasing age comes changes in the pharmacokinetics of 
medications. Medication absorption can be impaired by the age-
related decrease in small bowel surface area, slowing of gastric 
emptying, and increase in gastric pH. This decreased acidity can 
cause the early release of enteric-coated medications. The relatively 
increased fat volume of older adults also increases the distribution 
of lipophilic medications, increasing their half-lives and potential 
for toxicity. Hepatically metabolized medications through the cyto-
chrome P450 system also require titration in the geriatric popula-
tion, as first-pass metabolism has been shown to decrease by about 
1% per year after age 40 years. Renal excretion diminishes with age 
as the glomerular filtration rate also declines, causing the need for 
close monitoring of renally metabolized pharmaceuticals.
40
Medication Reconciliation
Each encounter with patients is an opportunity to review their 
medication profile to deprescribe, downtitrate, or further educate 
them about proper use of their medications. In the inpatient set -
ting, this can be done in conjunction with the rehabilitation 
team’s pharmacist. Polypharmacy is typically defined as taking five 
or more prescription drug treatments and is associated with in -
creased mortality, disability, hospitalizations, falls, cognitive and 
functional impairment, adverse drug events, and worsened adher-
ence to a medication regimen.
22,39 As of 2018, about 44% of the 
US geriatric population fall into this category, which has been 
trending upward.
54 Particular attention should be paid to medica-
tions that have central nervous system or cognitive effects, such as 
antidepressants, opioids, antiepileptics, benzodiazepines, medica -
tions with anticholinergic properties, and sleeping pills. These 
should be evaluated in the context of necessity, renal and hepatic 
function, efficacy, and possible interactions with the patient’s 
other medications.
The Beers criteria is a set of guidelines created by the American 
Geriatrics Society that is regularly updated and can help guide 
medication choices in the geriatric population. The guideline 
specifies potentially harmful medications for older adults, medica-
tions with significant side effects associated with certain health 
conditions, and medications that interact with other medications 
commonly taken by this age group. This guideline can be utilized 
during a reconciliation to advise prescribing choices.
6
Pain Management
Pain, both acute and chronic, is addressed by physiatrists in both 
the inpatient and outpatient settings. In the geriatric population, 
pain is associated with reduced mobility, increased anxiety and 
depression, and disruption of social relationships.
20 Pain manage-
ment should be approached in a multidisciplinary fashion with 
escalation from nonpharmacologic (physical and occupational 
therapies, early mobilization, environmental modifications, psy-
chological interventions) to pharmacologic and, finally, invasive 
interventions. When pharmacologic therapy is considered, non -
centrally acting medications should be utilized when indicated. 
For example, providers should consider the use of calcitonin for 
vertebral compression fractures
10 rather than opioids or nonste -
roidal antiinflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). When opioids or

641Chapter 31  Geriatrics
NSAIDs are the indicated treatment of choice, consideration 
should be given to the difference in pharmacodynamics and ki -
netics noted earlier, which may lead to decreased tolerance and an 
increased risk for adverse drug reactions. For opioids, this includes 
constipation, sedation, and increased falls risk. NSAID use in 
older adults is associated with gastric bleeding and ulceration, 
renal dysfunction, myocardial infarction, and stroke.
21
Mentation
Delirium
Delirium is commonly encountered in the inpatient acute care 
and rehabilitation settings. It is defined as “an acute, fluctuating 
syndrome of altered attention, awareness, and cognition” often 
seen in the geriatric population.
25 Delirium has up to a 60% 
prevalence in the acute rehabilitation setting, with many cases 
likely going undiagnosed.
25 Delirium can prevent meaningful 
participation in a rehabilitation program and cause significant 
decline in function. Addressing delirium starts with prevention 
techniques that all members of the care team can help to employ 
(Box 31.1).
Diagnosis of delirium can be made using criteria from the 
Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders , Fifth Edi-
tion.
7 Screening for delirium should occur on initial patient en -
counter and periodically throughout the patient’s rehabilitation 
stay. The Brief Interview for Mental Status is a tool used to deter-
mine a baseline level of cognition by quickly assessing a patient’s 
repetition, temporal orientation, and recall. It can be repeated to 
show any fluctuation in this baseline that may raise concern for 
developing delirium or, on a longer term, existence of dementia.
30 
The Confusion Assessment Method is a validated instrument and 
diagnostic algorithm for the identification of delirium and is 
available in both long (10 questions) and short (4 questions) 
forms. T ogether, these tools can be used by any member of the 
care team to potentially identify delirium early. Workup and treat-
ment for delirium should include medication reconciliation and 
assessment of causative etiologies, such as infection or electrolyte 
derangements. T reatment of the underlying cause is the initial 
goal followed by supportive care and prevention of worsening 
until delirium clears. This involves ensuring adequate nutrition 
and hydration, enforcement of sleep patterns, assuring pain man-
agement, and, if absolutely necessary, pharmacologic manage -
ment with antipsychotics, benzodiazepines, or antidepressants.
25
Dementia
Dementia is a collective term describing a pathology that affects 
memory, cognition, and communication and causing increased 
difficulty with motor and coordination functions. The most com-
mon types of dementia are Alzheimer disease, vascular dementia, 
Lewy body disease, frontotemporal lobar degeneration, and Par-
kinson dementia.
2 These are typically considered irreversible and 
often progressive. The role of the physiatrist in dementia involves 
early recognition of its onset and the development of an interven-
tional plan to address the progressive deficits the patient will face. 
This often involves a multidisciplinary team consisting of physical 
and occupational therapists, speech therapists, and neuropsy-
chologists. T ogether, cognition, language, and mobility can be 
bolstered by the team approach prior to the patient’s decline, or 
compensatory strategies can be developed once skills and abilities 
begin to diminish.
Depression
Depression can develop acutely in the geriatric age group or con -
tinue chronically from early ages. Depression leads to worsened 
in-hospital health status and lower rates of improvement both in 
the acute and postacute settings.
12 At times, the causes of this 
depression are related directly to musculoskeletal impairments 
leading to lack of mobility, pain, and loss of independence. The 
physiatrist can play a direct role in treating all these problems. It 
is also essential to ensure patients are provided with appropriate 
referrals for follow-up with a mental health professional.
Mobility
Maintaining mobility is essential for healthy aging because it has 
direct beneficial effects on physical, neurologic, and mental health 
quality of life in the geriatric population. Physiologic changes in 
the elderly, frailty, and falls are key components of geriatric mobil-
ity and are discussed elsewhere in this chapter.
Mobility Screening
Mobility can be assessed using a number of validated screening 
measures. Examples include the timed up and go test, SPPB, dy -
namic gait index, 6-minute walk test, 5-time sit to stand test, and 
Berg balance scale.
45 These can be performed in both inpatient 
and outpatient settings by physiatrists, nursing, or therapy teams. 
The clinician’s choice of test depends on the purpose of the assess-
ment, such as determining risk of falls at home, assessing the 
ability to perform a task-specific activity, or measuring endurance. 
These measures exist in the physiatrist’s toolbox for patient assess-
ments, and outcomes can be tracked over time to guide treat -
ments and recommendations.
Adaptive Equipment
Durable medical equipment is used to address mobility concerns 
in all age groups for both chronic and acute causes of mobility 
limitations. In the elderly, equipment recommendations should 
consider the patient’s individualized needs and abilities, including 
coordination and cognitive function, home environment, avail -
able assistance, and insurance coverage. For example, if a walker is 
deemed necessary within the home, consideration should be given 
to the patient’s ability to negotiate stairs in the home while toting 
the walker. If unable, the patient should be recommended to have 
a walker on each floor of the home. Straight single-point canes or 
quad canes are relatively lightweight and convenient devices that 
are used in one hand and provide proprioceptive feedback and 
Delirium Prevention Recommendations
• Promote periods of continuous sleep
• Use frequent reorienta tion techniques
• Promote moving pa tient out of bed at least three times a day for meals 
when possible
• Encoura ge visual and hearing aids if there is any impairment present
• Encoura ge family members at bedside
• Avoid medica tions that may worsen delirium
• Anticholinergics
• Antihistamines
• Benzodiazepines
• Opioids
• Avoid physical restraints unless pa tient is a danger to self or others
• Utilize one-to-one obser vation if necessary
• Remove indwelling ca theters and minimize lines as much as medically  
possible
• BOX 31.1

SECTION 3  Common Clinical Problems
increased stability while offloading a potentially painful hip or 
knee. Walkers provide a greater degree of stability but require the 
use of both arms and have increased weight and size when com -
pared to a cane. A rollator can be useful in the more mobile pa -
tient because it provides a consistent place to sit and rest when 
needed. However, rollators can cause falls if the brakes are used 
incorrectly or if the device, which is on wheels, moves too quickly 
for the patient to keep up with. A wheelchair should be consid -
ered to allow for longer distance mobilization outside the home. 
Reliance on a wheelchair can lead to disuse atrophy and further 
immobilization; thus active mobility should always be encour -
aged. A combination of adaptive devices is often the correct solu-
tion for short- vs. long-distance ambulation as well as home vs. 
community ambulation.
Orthotics
The use of foot orthoses in the elderly is helpful for the treatment 
of foot, leg, or back problems. They redistribute pressure, provide 
cushion, and modify alignment of the feet and legs. They have 
been shown to improve postural stability and lower extremity 
function. This provides an inexpensive and easy-to-implement 
recommendation that improves balance and reduces falls. Foot 
orthoses can be acquired over the counter or through custom 
molding, which would require referral to a podiatrist. 33
What Really Matters
Arguably, the most important M in the 4Ms framework is deter -
mining what matters to each individual patient. Physiatrists, 
through their innately holistic approach to patient care, are well 
positioned to guide patients in identifying their needs, prefer -
ences, and goals. Health-related quality of life can be assessed us -
ing several different instruments and is fundamentally patients’ 
perception and evaluation of their own health and its effect on 
their daily living. It can be useful to identify areas of potential 
improvement that physiatrists can target using the interdisciplin -
ary team at their disposal. This may include mobility concerns, for 
which physical and occupational therapy programs can be crafted, 
or pain, for which pharmacologic or noninterventional modalities 
can be prescribed. Through these assessments, physiatrists can 
help enhance the overall quality of life for geriatric patients.
Exercise Recommendation and Sports 
Injuries in Older Adults
The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention recommends 
that every week, adults age 65 or older need to engage in  
(1) aerobic physical activities of at least 150 minutes of moderate 
intensity (e.g., 30 min/day, 5 days/week) or 75 minutes of vigor -
ous intensity or equivalent combination of moderate intensity 
and vigorous intensity, (2) at least 2 days of activities that 
strengthen muscles, and (3) activities to improve balance. 50 Mul-
ticomponent physical activities with all three components are 
some sports, tai chi, and gardening. As the population ages, the 
number of aging athletes also increases. Older adults report that 
the main drivers for playing sports are maintaining their health 
and well-being, socializing and feelings of positivity, developing 
new relationships and strengthening existing ones, competition, 
and achievement.48 Pickleball is the fastest-growing sport in the 
United States, according to the Sports and Fitness Industry As -
sociation, and it is joining the list of age-friendly sports, such as 
golf and swimming. The strategies to prevent sports-related inju -
ries include maintenance of joint flexibility, allowing sufficient 
recovery intervals between plays, and paying attention to the early 
signs of injuries (“listen to your body”). Unusual discomfort, 
tingling, numbness, weakness, or limited range of motion are 
early signs of potential injuries or disorders. In addition, older 
adults who remain flexible and open-minded about the choice  
of sports and are willing to consider transitioning to relatively 
low-impact sports seek to continue enjoying sports activities for 
their lifetime. The American Council on Exercise recommends 
drinking 17 to 20 ounces of water 2 hours before working out,  
7 to 10 ounces every 10 to 20 minutes, and 16 to 24 ounces for 
every pound of body weight lost. 5
Assistive Technologies for the Geriatric 
Population
Assistive technology (AT) is defined as any equipment, software 
program, or product system that is used to improve the functional 
capabilities of people with or without disabilities. AT includes 
both low- and high-tech solutions. Often older adults and their 
caregivers do not have an adequate knowledge of what ATs are 
available, how to use them, and where to find training and sup -
port.38 The Assistive T echnology Act T echnical Assistance and 
T raining Center (AT3 Center) was created as a sponsored project 
of Assistive T echnology Act Programs with the mission of increas-
ing access to and acquisition of AT by individuals with disabilities 
across their lifespan.34 The AT3 Center website (https://at3center.
net/) lists the resources about the AT Act Program in each state. 
Low-tech AT includes tools offered to improve functional mobil-
ity or ADLs: chair lift with handles, walking aids, reacher, toilet 
aids, and bed handles. High-tech devices include alarm monitor -
ing devices for medical emergencies, telehealth systems with sen -
sors monitoring vital health information and communicating 
with providers, GPS trackers for individuals at risk for wandering, 
and various memory aids reminding patients about essential items 
(e.g., keys), taking medications, or feeding their pets.
There are many personal alarm products for people to call for 
help. In choosing the right device for an individual, one should 
consider the feature that allows the wearer to talk to the monitor-
ing team through the device, automatic fall detection, GPS capa -
bility, the requirement for internet or landline connectivity, battery 
life, and the cost structure (rental, monthly fee).
Smart home technology allows users to program and control 
household appliances, door locks, and other devices at home. 
Voice control adds convenience to smart home systems. Several 
states have smart home demonstration sites, including North 
Dakota, Oklahoma, Illinois, and Alaska. Smart home monitoring 
systems with home sensors can detect any unusual activities or 
inactivity of people in their homes and update the users’ caregiv-
ers. Additional features include the locking of gates, virtual 
monitoring of users, and specific notifications for opening medi -
cation cabinets. Several ATs for cognitive dysfunction have been 
studied with mixed results. For example, a pilot trial of a wearable 
camera that informs the user of a visitor’s name and relationship 
found no evidence of improvements in social engagement or qual-
ity of life. 32 Social stigma, AT reliability, and system feasibility 
have been identified as barriers to meaningful use. 32
The American Association of Retired Persons (AARP) featured 
several technologies that may help people maintain their indepen-
dence in their own homes for the future: (1) artificial intelligence

to answer questions, (2) telehealth, (3) smart cars with driver 
safety technologies and blind-spot warnings, and (4) robots to 
assist around the house (e.g., Labrador Retriever). The AgeT ech 
Collaborative from AARP was launched with a global mission to 
improve people’s lives as they age.1 Through this startup collabora-
tion, forward-thinking investors, enterprises, and testbeds come 
together to bring user-centered technology innovation to the 
market.
Ethical Decision Making
Biomedical ethics is the branch of applied ethics that studies the 
philosophical, social, and legal issues arising in medicine and the 
life sciences. It is comprised of four principles 53:
• Autonomy: Patients have the power to make rational decisions 
and moral choices, and each patient should be allowed to have 
self-determination.
• Nonmaleficence: The physician is obligated to not harm the 
patient.
• Beneficence: The physician is obligated to act for the patient’s 
benefit.
• Justice: Patients should receive fair, equitable, and appropriate 
treatment.
Biomedical ethics issues often arise when these principles come 
into conflict. Factors such as informed consent and capacity can 
also play a part in ethical conflicts. Many hospitals have ethics 
committees and ethics consults available to support resolution of 
these conflicts. In geriatric patients, these conflicts often come to 
the forefront during end-of-life care.
The ethical decision-making model at a glance consists of 17:
 1. Identify the problem.
 2. Apply the American Counseling Association’s Code of Ethics.
 3. Determine the nature and dimensions of the dilemma.
 4. Generate potential courses of action.
 5. Consider the potential consequences of all options and deter -
mine a course of action.
 6. Evaluate the selected course of action.
 7. Implement the course of action
Summary
Care of the geriatric population has never been more important, 
and it is critical that the physiatrist understand some of the spe -
cific problems that commonly impact this group. Sarcopenia, 
frailty, osteoporosis, falls, arthritis, and neurologic conditions are
